section: Temperature dependence of the deformation behavior and mechanical properties of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel for cryogenic application

context_update.summary: The current chunk introduces the research on the temperature-dependent deformation and mechanical properties of an Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel. Tensile and Charpy impact tests were conducted at −196 °C, −77 °C and room temperature. Key abstract findings: (1) lower temperature reduces SFE, promotes planar glide and increases LAGB fraction, improving both tensile strength and elongation; (2) lower impact temperature raises crack initiation resistance but accelerates the transition to unstable crack propagation, reducing crack propagation energy and resulting in the lowest Charpy absorbed energy at −196 °C; (3) fractography shows a smaller fracture area, larger fibrous zone and more ductile features at higher temperature, and secondary cracks at RT delay fracture to improve toughness. The initial microstructure is fully austenitic as shown by EBSD and XRD (Fig. 1). This chunk lacks quantitative property data and processing parameters; subsequent chunks are expected to provide detailed experimental methods and results. The material composition and test variable (temperature) should be retained.
microstructure: initial fully austenitic structure
low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) increased by planar glide at low temperature
properties: tensile strength and elongation increase with decreasing temperature
higher crack initiation resistance at low temperature
lower crack propagation resistance at low temperature
mechanisms: SFE reduction with temperature promotes planar glide of dislocations
planar glide increases LAGB fraction
stable-to-unstable crack propagation transition faster at low temperature
secondary cracks at RT delay fracture

context_update.summary: The current chunk details experimental procedures for the Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si steel: vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1250 °C for 2 h, forging at 1100–950 °C. Tensile tests at −196, −77 °C and RT at 0.001 s⁻¹ with three samples each yielded engineering and true stress–strain curves (Fig. 2) and strength-elongation data. Yield strength, UTS, TE and UE all increase with decreasing temperature; empirical linear relations are UTS = 813.0 – 2.36 T and YS = 474.6 – 2.05 T (T in °C). EBSD of deformed tensile specimens (Fig. 3) shows LAGB fraction rises from 56.0% (RT) to 70.2% (−196 °C) and mechanical twins are present at all temperatures. Charpy impact test setup (V-notch, 10×10×55 mm, at same three temperatures) and post-test characterization (3D microscopy, SEM, ion-polished cross-section EBSD with 0.8 μm step) are described but no impact data yet. TEM sample preparation uses 6% perchloric acid + 94% glacial acetic acid electrolyte. Key figures to retain: Fig. 2 (tensile curves and strength-temperature relations) and Fig. 3 (EBSD maps with GB fractions and GND distributions). The next chunks should provide detailed deformation mechanisms, fracture behavior, and impact toughness data.
processing_route: vacuum induction melting in 50 kg furnace
microstructure: initial fully austenitic
deformed: LAGB fraction 70.2% (−196 °C), 63.0% (−77 °C), 56.0% (RT)
HAGB fraction 29.8%, 37.0%, 44.0%
mechanical twins present in all conditions
GND distribution maps obtained
properties: true stress–strain and work hardening rate curves (Fig. 2b)
work hardening rate decreases with strain
mechanisms: deformation twinning at all temperatures
lower temperature promotes planar slip, raising LAGB fraction

context_update.summary: This chunk reports initial fully austenitic equiaxed microstructure (Fig. 1a,b), a measured density of 7.114 g/cm³, and XRD evidence for stable austenite with no isothermal martensite at −196 °C (Fig. 1c). Tensile stress–strain curves (Fig. 2) show that yield and ultimate tensile strengths increase linearly with decreasing temperature, while total and uniform elongation also rise; work hardening rate becomes near-linear at −77 and −196 °C but continuously decreases at RT, indicating a negative temperature dependence of strength. EBSD of deformed microstructures (Fig. 3) reveals that decreasing temperature sharply raises the LAGB fraction, concentrates LAGBs in finer grains, and causes GND accumulation at LAGBs. TEM confirms mechanical twins at −196 °C (Fig. 4a) and {111} microbands characteristic of planar glide at −77 °C (Fig. 4b). Charpy impact data (Fig. 5) provide energy partitions (unstable, stable, initiation) for −196, −77 and RT; fracture surfaces show dimple-dominated features (Fig. 6). These results establish temperature-dependent microstructure–property linkages and supply essential evidence for subsequent discussion on deformation mechanisms and cryogenic toughness.
microstructure: initial: fully austenitic equiaxed grains, no isothermal martensite at -196 °C
deformed: LAGB fraction increases with decreasing temperature, LAGBs concentrate in fine grains
GNDs accumulate at LAGBs
-196 °C deformation: mechanical twins on {111} along <112>
-77 °C deformation: microbands on {111}, no twins
Charpy fracture: dimples, large dimples/tear edges
properties: tensile: TE and UE increase, YS and UTS linearly increase with decreasing temperature
work hardening rate increases with decreasing temperature; near-linear at -77 and -196 °C, continuous decline at RT
Charpy energy partitions (Fig. 5): -196 °C 40/30/110 J; -77 °C 50/180/65 J; RT 60/180/55 J
mechanisms: lower temperature reduces SFE, promoting planar glide
planar glide leads to mechanical twins (-196 °C) or microbands (-77 °C) and raises LAGB fraction
GND accumulation at LAGBs indicates severe local deformation

exact tensile values (YS, UTS, TE, UE)
quantitative crack propagation/initiation details beyond absorbed energy

context_update.summary: This chunk presents Charpy impact energies: 327 J at RT, 277 J at -77 °C, and 188 J at -196 °C. Low-temperature decrease is linked to increased activation energy limiting planar slip. Fractography (Fig. 6) shows shear lips on all samples but a less fibrous zone and flatter 3D surface at -196 °C; -196 °C features large dimples and tear edges (quasi-cleavage), while ductile features dominate at -77 °C and RT. Stacking fault energy is calculated as 32, 40, and 61 mJ/m² at -196 °C, -77 °C, and RT (Table 1). SFE-dependent deformation mechanisms are discussed: region I wavy glide, region II planar glide (twins/glide bands), region III DIMT. For this steel, RT and -77 °C SFE suppress DIMT/twinning, but -196 °C SFE permits twinning. Work hardening has two stages (n1, n2); n1 rises with decreasing temperature because of enhanced planar glide and suppressed dynamic recovery, and microbands/twins boost hardening. Key figures: Fig. 5–10 (load-deflection, fracture, SFE-elongation, lnσ-lnε, impact characteristic, EBSD near fracture). Subsequent content may analyze impact toughness and fracture mechanisms.
key_variables: fracture surface features (shear lips, fibrous zone, dimples, tear edges, quasi-cleavage)
microstructure: shear lips on all impact fracture surfaces
fibrous zone nearly absent at -196 °C, larger at -77 °C and RT
quasi-cleavage fracture with high-density large dimples and tear edges at -196 °C
ductile dimple morphology at -77 °C and RT
flatter fracture surface at -196 °C by 3D topography
dislocation cells from wavy glide; glide bands and mechanical twins from planar glide
properties: work hardening exponent n1 increases with decreasing temperature
mechanisms: increased activation energy limits slip system activation and causes early low-temperature fracture
high SFE → wavy glide → dislocation cells → low elongation
moderate SFE → planar glide → twins/glide bands → high work hardening
low SFE → DIMT → high strength but brittleness
for high Mn steels, SFE 18–35 mJ/m² favours mechanical twinning
experimental steel: RT and -77 °C SFE suppress DIMT and twinning; -196 °C SFE permits twinning
reduced SFE promotes planar glide, suppresses cross-slip, reduces dynamic recovery → higher n1
microbands and mechanical twins act as barriers enhancing work hardening
figures_or_tables: Fig. 6a-f (fracture surfaces, shear lips, fibrous zone, 3D topography, dimples)
Fig. 9 (smoothed impact load-deflection curves, characteristic values)

context_update.summary: This chunk details the deformation mechanisms (Section 4.1) and cryogenic impact toughness (Section 4.2) of the experimental Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si steel. Work hardening is divided into two stages: n1 increases at low temperature due to reduced SFE enhancing planar glide and suppressing dynamic recovery; microbands and mechanical twins further boost hardening. Charpy impact tests at −196 °C, −77 °C and RT are evaluated using ISO procedures. Characteristic forces Fgy, Fm, Fin, and ratio Fiu/Fm are analyzed, showing enhanced crack initiation resistance but faster unstable crack propagation at lower temperatures. Absorbed energies are quantified: Winitiation = 112, 68, 57 J for −196 °C, −77 °C, RT, while Wstable and Wunstable decrease with temperature, lowering overall toughness. EBSD of fracture cross-sections reveals transgranular fracture with zigzag paths and local orientation gradients; secondary cracks at RT relieve stress and improve toughness. SEM shows limited planar glide at −196 °C, explaining poor low-temperature crack propagation resistance. Conclusions summarize strength–ductility synergy and toughness trade-offs. Subsequent analysis may integrate these findings with earlier microstructure and tensile data.
key_variables: strain rate (low for tensile, high for impact)
Charpy impact characteristic forces (Fgy, Fm, Fin, Fiu/Fm, RC)
microstructure: transgranular fracture with zigzag crack paths
local orientation gradient near cracks
secondary cracks at RT
planar glide features (limited at −196 °C)
properties: Fgy negative temperature dependence, Fm increases with decreasing temperature
Wstable and Wunstable decrease with decreasing temperature
mechanisms: reduced SFE enhances planar glide, inhibits cross-slip, increases n1
microbands and mechanical twins enhance work hardening
secondary cracks release stress concentration, delay fracture
limited planar glide at low temperature reduces crack propagation resistance

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: Subsequent content may analyze impact toughness and fracture mechanisms. This chunk details the deformation mechanisms (Section 4.1) and cryogenic impact toughness (Section 4.2) of the experimental Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si steel. Work hardening is divided into two stages: n1 increases at low temperature due to reduced SFE enhancing planar glide and suppressing dynamic recovery; microbands and mechanical twins further boost hardening. Charpy impact tests at −196 °C, −77 °C and RT are evaluated using ISO procedures. Characteristic forces Fgy, Fm, Fin, and ratio Fiu/Fm are analyzed, showing enhanced crack initiation resistance but faster unstable crack propagation at lower temperatures. Absorbed energies are quantified: Winitiation = 112, 68, 57 J for −196 °C, −77 °C, RT, while Wstable and Wunstable decrease with temperature, lowering overall toughness. EBSD of fracture cross-sections reveals transgranular fracture with zigzag paths and local orientation gradients; secondary cracks at RT relieve stress and improve toughness. SEM shows limited planar glide at −196 °C, explaining poor low-temperature crack propagation resistance. Conclusions summarize strength–ductility synergy and toughness trade-offs. Subsequent analysis may integrate these findings with earlier microstructure and tensile data.
Material systems: Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel; fully austenitic (γ) phase
Sample aliases: experimental steel
Processing route: vacuum induction melting in 50 kg furnace; air cool to RT; homogenization 1250 °C / 2 h; forging 1100–950 °C to 60 mm diameter rounds; furnace cool to RT; vacuum induction melting; homogenization at 1250 °C for 2 h; forging from 1100 °C to 950 °C
Key variables: test temperature (−196 °C, −77 °C, RT); tensile vs. impact loading; test temperature: −196 °C, −77 °C, RT; strain rate: 0.001 s⁻¹; tensile loading vs Charpy impact; test temperature (-196 °C, -77 °C, RT); Charpy impact test temperatures: -196 °C, -77 °C, RT; SFE values: 32, 40, 61 mJ/m²
Microstructure: initial fully austenitic structure; low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) increased by planar glide at low temperature; initial fully austenitic; deformed: LAGB fraction 70.2% (−196 °C), 63.0% (−77 °C), 56.0% (RT); HAGB fraction 29.8%, 37.0%, 44.0%; mechanical twins present in all conditions; GND distribution maps obtained; initial: fully austenitic equiaxed grains, no isothermal martensite at -196 °C
Properties: tensile strength and elongation increase with decreasing temperature; lowest Charpy absorbed energy at −196 °C; higher crack initiation resistance at low temperature; lower crack propagation resistance at low temperature; engineering stress–strain curves (Fig. 2a); true stress–strain and work hardening rate curves (Fig. 2b); UTS = 813.0 – 2.36 T MPa; YS = 474.6 – 2.05 T MPa
